constraints: Never skip orders unless no feasible assignment exists.
Rollback tentative insertions when a candidate route fails.
Call RenewnTotalCost before returning Dispatch.
Avoid negative, suspiciously low, timeout, and missing-result candidates.
summary: SA baseline or seed heuristic for safe InsertShips generation.
title: SA baseline InsertShips extracted from project root main.go

constraints: Never skip orders; fall back to force-insert when no feasible position exists
Rollback tentative insertions before trying next candidate position
Call RenewnTotalCost() exactly once before returning Dispatch
GenRoute() after every successful AddShip
Only try new Assign creation when AssignsLen < MAXASSIGNS
content:
Algorithm: Nearest Insertion (Rosenkrantz, Stearns & Lewis 1977)
Core idea: For each order in sequence, try every existing Assign and pick the one with lowest incremental cost. Simple greedy local optimization — always choose the nearest feasible position.

Pseudocode:
FOR each order k (0 to len(oris)-1):
  best_assign = -1; best_delta = +Inf
  FOR a = 0 to AssignsLen-1:
    Save Assign[a] state
    ok = Assigns[a].AddShip(total_ship+k, oris[k], dess[k])
    IF ok: Assigns[a].GenRoute(); delta = new_Cost - saved_Cost
    IF delta < best_delta: best_delta = delta; best_assign = a
    Rollback: Assigns[a].RemoveShip(total_ship+k); Assigns[a].GenRoute()
  IF best_assign >= 0:
    Apply insertion to Assigns[best_assign]
  ELSE:
    Fallback: try force-insert into any Assign or create new one (if AssignsLen < MAXASSIGNS)
dispatch.RenewnTotalCost(); RETURN dispatch

InsertShips mapping:
- oris[k]/dess[k] = dynamic order pair
- Assign.AddShip() = feasibility check; returns false if route capacity exceeded
- Cost delta via GenRoute() approximates insertion distance
- Undo pattern: save state → try → evaluate → restore before next candidate
- Suitable for low-density (d25): few orders, simple greedy usually sufficient
summary: For each order, try all existing assignments and pick the position with lowest cost delta. Greedy, local, deterministic.
title: Nearest Insertion Heuristic (Rosenkrantz, Stearns & Lewis, 1977)

constraints: Never skip orders; farthest-first is a ordering strategy, not a skip rule
Rollback tentative insertions before trying next candidate
Call RenewnTotalCost() before return
Farthest criterion uses cal_dis(StationCurrent, ori) as distance proxy
Fallback when no feasible insertion: force-insert into any available Assign
content:
Algorithm: Farthest Insertion (Rosenkrantz et al. 1977)
Core idea: Prioritize the order farthest from any current route first, then insert at its best-cost position. Strategy: tackle the hardest-to-serve orders early when routes still have flexibility.

Pseudocode:
WHILE unserved orders remain:
  // Find farthest unserved order from any route
  farthest_k = -1; farthest_dist = -1
  FOR each unserved k:
    min_dist = min_{a} cal_dis(Assigns[a].StationCurrent, oris[k])
    IF min_dist > farthest_dist: farthest_dist = min_dist; farthest_k = k
  // Insert farthest_k at best-cost position
  best_assign = -1; best_delta = +Inf
  FOR a = 0 to AssignsLen-1:
    Save state; try AddShip; if ok: GenRoute; record delta; undo
  IF best_assign >= 0: apply insertion
  ELSE: fallback force-insert
  Mark farthest_k as served
dispatch.RenewnTotalCost(); RETURN dispatch

InsertShips mapping:
- Order selection uses cal_dis(StationCurrent, oris[k]) as distance from route
- Complementary to nearest insertion: nearest = easy-first, farthest = hard-first
- Suitable for dispersed distributions or when d25 nearest insertion produces balanced but suboptimal routes
summary: Select the unserved order farthest from any route first, then insert at best-cost position. Handles dispersed orders better than nearest-first.
title: Farthest Insertion Heuristic (Rosenkrantz et al., 1977)

constraints: Never skip orders; fallback to seed-style when no feasible insertion found
Rollback tentative insertions before scoring next candidate
Call RenewnTotalCost() before return
For this problem (no time windows), simplified weights: w1=1, w2=0, w3=0 (reward far-from-depot orders)
At high density, set α2>0 to penalize insertion distance
content:
Algorithm: Solomon I1 Sequential Insertion (Solomon 1987, Operations Research 35(2):254-265)
Core insertion criterion: score(i,u,j) = w1·d(0,u) - w2·c11(i,u,j) - w3·c12(i,u,j)
  d(0,u) = distance from depot to order u (reward for serving far orders)
  c11 = d(i,u) + d(u,j) - μ·d(i,j) (extra travel distance, μ=1)
  c12 = time push-forward at j (≈0 for InsertShips, no strict time windows)
  Simplified (α1=1, α2=0, α3=0): maximize d(0,u) subject to feasible insertion

Pseudocode (simplified for InsertShips):
WHILE unserved not empty:
  best_score = -Inf; best_action = None
  FOR each unserved k:
    d_depot = cal_dis(Station{X:0,Y:0}, oris[k])
    FOR a = 0 to AssignsLen-1:
      Save state; ok = Assigns[a].AddShip(...)
      IF ok: Assigns[a].GenRoute(); c11 = new_Cost - saved_Cost
      score = α1·d_depot - α2·c11
      IF score > best_score: best_score = score; best_action = (k,a)
      Rollback
  IF best_action: execute insertion
  ELSE: fallback force-insert any unserved order
dispatch.RenewnTotalCost(); RETURN dispatch

InsertShips mapping:
- Selection criterion (which order next) and insertion criterion (where) combined into single score
- Simplified weights suitable for InsertShips since no time windows exist
- At higher density (d50-d75): reduce α1 or increase α2 to penalize insertion cost more heavily
summary: Standard VRPTW construction: select order and position maximizing weighted savings = w1·d0u - w2·c11 - w3·c12.
title: Solomon I1 Sequential Insertion Heuristic (Solomon, 1987)

constraints: Never skip orders; regret-based ordering, not skip logic
Rollback tentative insertions before scoring
Call RenewnTotalCost() before return
Single-option orders get INF regret = insert immediately
Fallback force-insert when no feasible insertion exists for any remaining order
content:
Algorithm: Regret-2 Insertion (Potvin & Rousseau 1993, EJOR 66(3):331-340)
Core concept: regret(u) = cost_2nd_best(u) - cost_1st_best(u)
Select the order with MAXIMUM regret — if we don't insert it now with its best option, the second-best option is much worse (or doesn't exist). Prevents leaving hard orders to last.

Pseudocode:
WHILE unserved not empty:
  best_regret = -1; best_action = None
  FOR each unserved k:
    costs = []
    FOR a = 0 to AssignsLen-1:
      Save state; ok = Assigns[a].AddShip(...)
      IF ok: Assigns[a].GenRoute(); costs.append((a, new_Cost - saved_Cost))
      Rollback
    IF len(costs) == 0: CONTINUE (no feasible position — will fallback)
    IF len(costs) == 1: regret = INF (only one option — must insert now)
    ELSE: sort costs by delta; regret = costs[1].delta - costs[0].delta
    IF regret > best_regret: best_regret = regret; best_action = (k, costs[0].assign)
  IF best_action: execute insertion for best_regret_order
  ELSE: fallback force-insert any unserved order
dispatch.RenewnTotalCost(); RETURN dispatch

InsertShips mapping:
- Regret = cost of not taking the best option now. High regret → insert immediately
- Each Assign represents a route; insertion positions within Assign map to route positions
- Key advantage: avoids greedy trap where easy orders get good positions and hard orders get leftovers
- Regret-2 uses top-2 best positions; Regret-k generalizes to top-k
summary: Select order with max regret = (2nd-best insertion cost - best insertion cost). Handles hard-to-serve orders first, avoiding greedy trap.
title: Regret-2 Insertion Heuristic (Potvin & Rousseau, 1993)

constraints: Never skip orders; savings-based ordering only, fallback for unserved
Rollback tentative insertions when evaluating merge feasibility
Call RenewnTotalCost() before return
Savings matrix computed from cal_dis() distances; d(depot,k) uses Station{X:0,Y:0} as depot proxy
Capacity check: combined demand of merged route must not exceed vehicle capacity (when applicable)
content:
Algorithm: Clarke-Wright Savings (Clarke & Wright 1964, Operations Research 12(4):568-581)
Core savings formula: s(i,j) = d(depot,i) + d(depot,j) - d(i,j)
If s(i,j) > 0, serving i and j on the same route saves distance compared to separate routes.
Process savings in descending order, merging routes when feasible.

Pseudocode (adapted for InsertShips):
// Compute savings for all order pairs
savings = []
FOR i = 0 to len(oris)-1:
  d0i = cal_dis(Station{X:0,Y:0}, oris[i])
  FOR j = i+1 to len(oris)-1:
    d0j = cal_dis(Station{X:0,Y:0}, oris[j])
    dij = cal_dis(oris[i], oris[j])
    s = d0i + d0j - dij
    IF s > 0: savings.append((s, i, j))
Sort savings by s descending
served = set()
FOR (s, i, j) in savings:
  IF both served: continue
  IF both unserved: create new Assign, insert i then j (best-cost each)
  ELSE IF one served: add unserved one to the served one's Assign
FOR each unserved k: greedy best-cost insertion into any existing Assign
  // or create new Assign if needed
dispatch.RenewnTotalCost(); RETURN dispatch

InsertShips mapping:
- Savings guides which orders should share an Assign (= share a route)
- d(depot,k) = cal_dis({0,0}, oris[k]) as distance from imaginary depot
- Route merging logic: decision whether to create new Assign or reuse existing for a pair
- Suitable for medium-high density (d50-d75) where route consolidation has largest impact
summary: Compute savings s(i,j) = d0i + d0j - dij for all order pairs. Merge highest-savings pairs first, creating shared routes when beneficial.
title: Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm (Clarke & Wright, 1964)
